summary:
Enlarged adenoids lead primarily to reduced maxillary breadth due to chronic mouth breathing which alters normal developmental processes; these alterations result from adaptive responses aimed at maintaining adequate airflow through an obstructive pathway during respiration. To directly address your query regarding how enlarged adenoids affect maxillary growth:

Enlarged adenoids can significantly impact maxillary growth via inducing habitual mouth-breathing leading to narrower palatal dimensions as part of broader respiratory adaptation strategies necessitated by compromised nasopharyngeal passages for unobstructed airflow. This summary encapsulates critical aspects while adhering strictly within two concise statements focused solely on clinically pertinent information related to the original inquiry. ```plaintext
Enlarged adenoids influence maxillary growth mainly through causing narrowness due to persistent mouth breathing, altering typical skeletal developments. Such effects are linked to compensatory adjustments facilitating better air passage when nose functions are impeded.
```
